data.title: Wesdome Gold Mines: Where High-Grade Ounces Meet Top-Ranked Jurisdictions
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted with a C$ or A$ in front of the figure. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Q2 Production & Sales

Wesdome Gold Mines Ltd. (OTCQX:WDOFF) released its Q2 '25 results in August, reporting quarterly production of ~42,800 ounces of gold, a 3% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to a much softer quarter at Kiena in Quebec (~17,200 ounces vs. ~24,800 ounces), offsetting a better quarter at its Eagle River Mine in Ontario where output increased 33% year-over-year. Unfortunately, this has left Wesdome at risk of missing its initial guidance midpoint of 200,000 ounces, with just ~88,500 ounces year-to-date or just ~44% of its guidance midpoint.

This is unfortunate, given that the company was coming off two consecutive modest beats on its guidance midpoint in 2023 and 2024 under new leadership, with 2025 setting up to be a minor miss without a very strong performance out of Kiena in Q4 '25.

Digging into its operations a little closer, Eagle River put together a solid performance in Q2 '25, producing ~25,600 ounces at $1,394/oz AISC, up from ~19,300 ounces at $1,860/oz in the year-ago period. Increased production was driven by industry-leading grades of 16.9 G/T of gold (Q2 '24: 11.8 G/T of gold), as well as higher recoveries at 96.7%, with Wesdome noting that grades benefited from improved dilution control. Notably, these results were achieved despite a planned 18-day mill shut in Q2'25 that impacted throughput in the period.

Given the strong performance in H1'25 (~54,600 ounces), Wesdome increased Eagle River's guidance midpoint by 5,000 ounces to 110,000 ounces while also increasing guidance on grades (14.0 - 15.0 G/T of gold vs. 13.0 - 15.0 G/T of gold for year previously). And while the year-to-date grade of 16.2 G/T of gold implies lower grades in H2 '25, operations will benefit from increased throughput relative to Q2 '25 levels. As for the impressive performance that bet plans in H1, Wesdome called out higher grade reconciliation and increased ounces from the 300 Zone, benefiting from optimized stope design, improved execution and refined grade control.

As for cost performance at Eagle River, lower AISC ($1,394/oz vs. $1,860/oz) benefited from increased ounces sold vs. produced offset by higher sustaining capital and higher royalties. One of the more important take-aways from the quarter was below, with Wesdome stating that the better performance at Eagle River was largely execution driven, not chasing grades, an encouraging sign that optimization work here and investments in the asset are paying off.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

"Importantly, performance year-to-date reflects disciplined execution, not opportunistic grade chasing."

- Wesdome Q2 '25 Conference Call.

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Unfortunately, while investors couldn't have asked for a much better H1 out of Eagle River, Kiena materially underperformed. This was evidenced by production of just ~17,200 ounces at $1,720/oz AISC in Q2'25, down from ~24,800 ounces at $1,123/oz AISC. Lower production in the period was related to significantly lower grades (10.7 G/T of gold vs. 13.5 G/T of gold), lower recoveries and lower mill throughput. Wesdome noted that equipment constraints and limited access to stopes and underperformance in one high-grade stope were responsible for the weaker results.

Given the weak Q2 '25 performance combined with an already weak Q1 '25, Kiena is sitting at just ~33,900 ounces year-to-date at $1,567/oz AISC, a considerable deviation from original guidance of 95,000 ounces at $1,312/oz AISC using the midpoint. And with the asset tracking this far behind plans, the company chose to revise guidance lower to 80,000 to 90,000 ounces at higher costs of $1,488/oz.

As for tackling these issues, the company is adjusting its maintenance practices to better align with Kiena Deep's current risk profile, with it stating that an optimized shift schedule will ensure round-the-clock maintenance coverage. In addition to this, rental equipment has been brought in to improve its fleet and reduce the risk of downtime, and it has increased its spare parts inventory. Finally, Wesdome shared that it is bringing more people to critical areas of the mine to build redundancy and has opened an additional maintenance workshop underground.

The latter should allow for better equipment utilization rates and reduce the impact to the mining sequence, which impacted Q2 '25 results because of higher-grade stopes being deferred into the second half. On a negative note, Q3 '25 didn't start as cleanly as hoped with a planned 4-day shutdown for hoist maintenance morphed into ten days of downtime, impacting mine production.

While these results may not be very inspiring, nor is the guidance cut, there is reason to be optimistic about this asset's future and why it makes sense to ignore the weak H1 '25 results. For starters, Wesdome noted it expects a much better Q4 with enhanced maintenance practices, an increase in available mining horizons and recent changes to site leadership. And it's important to remember that Kiena is currently impacted by having a single mining area exacerbating any issues given the lack of operational flexibility.

Fortunately, Wesdome will have three mining horizons available by year-end. This will triple its available mining areas, with these new areas including Presqu'ile and Level 136. Not only will this translate to a material increase in mine production, but it will greatly improve operational flexibility, with a single stope or two not making or breaking its quarter as we saw in Q2 '25. Just as importantly, Wesdome will see a significant improvement in its materials handling with the Presqu'ile ramp, providing a second means of transportation for people and materials vs. solely its shaft currently.

These improvements, in addition to increased ventilation and welcome surprises from an exploration standpoint (like much higher grades than anticipated at the B Zone), are positioning this asset to materially outperform on output relative to its LOMP delivered to the market in 2021. For those unfamiliar, this mine plan pointed to average annual production of ~84,000 ounces per year over seven years, with peak production of ~120,000 ounces.

Under the current setup, Presqu'ile alone looks like it can add up to 30,000 ounces/year of incremental ounces. However, Presqu'ile and Kiena Deep will still only be utilizing ~60% of the 2,000 TPD permitted capacity at the mill, with Wesdome's larger goal being to get both mills operating at 80% of capacity, implying rates closer to 1,600 TPD for Kiena. And with no shortage of targets at depth and others accessible from the 33 Level exploration ramp, there's certainly the potential for this to be a 170,000 plus ounce per annum asset long-term, if not better.

As for progress at Presqu'ile, this new near-surface mine is expected to contribute up to 10,000 ounces this year and drilling of the first stopes at Presqu'ile hit visible gold in areas of high-grade block model designs, an encouraging sign in regards to modeling completed here. On a negative note, initial stope production at Presqu'ile has been revised from "late 2025 or Q1 2026" vs. "by the end of 2025" previously. This is hardly a deal-breaker but is a minor change in schedule vs. prior expectations, suggesting that we may see slightly lower grades in Q1'26 vs. prior expectations for the larger Kiena Complex if Presqu'ile is still reliant on development ore for an extra couple of months.

Looking at the disappointing Q2 '25 cost performance with AISC spiking 53% year-over-year to $1,720/oz, this was related to much higher sustaining capital year-over-year (C$15.3 million vs. C$9.8 million) primarily related to increase exploration and mine capital equipment costs, and fewer ounces sold, with fixed costs divided over a much smaller denominator. However, Kiena should look much different in Q4 '25 as production ramps up and with the benefit of new ounces from Presqu'ile, leveraging its fixed cost base and bringing AISC back below $1,400/oz, with a further improvement in AISC next year as stope ore is available from Presqu'ile.

As for project costs, Wesdome noted that exploration ramp development and associated infrastructure is trending higher than budget, though partially related to scope expansion in order to increase ventilation capacity.

Financial Results

As for Wesdome's financial results, revenue jumped 63% year-over-year to ~C$209 million, operating cash flow soared to ~C$101 million (+77% year-over-year) and free cash flow increased to ~C$53 million despite increased capex spend year-over-year. Revenue and operating cash flow benefited from higher ounces sold than produced (~45,900 vs. ~42,800) and a record average realized gold price of $3,279/oz, while free cash flow benefited from a slight under-spend on capex year-to-date (C$80 million vs. C$190 million).

Fortunately, increased capex in the back half will be offset by higher production levels and continued strength in the gold price (XAUUSD:CUR), translating to another two very strong quarters of free cash flow ahead. As for Wesdome's balance sheet, it remains pristine despite the increased spending at both sites this year and C$33.5 million in cash related to the Angus Gold acquisition. In fact, Wesdome is sitting on ~C$188 million in cash and no debt. Notably, Wesdome increased its RCF to US$250 million with an additional US$50 million accordion, giving the over C$500 million in liquidity.

The last point worth noting is that Wesdome shared that its strong balance sheet has prompted it to look at a disciplined capital return to shareholders while balancing spend on exploration and growth, suggesting investors could see dividends or buybacks at some point in 2026. Assuming the company looked at paying a dividend and even calibrating to a more conservative gold price assumption (FY2026 estimates at $3,250/oz gold: ~$200 million) and a very conservative payout ratio of just 15% of free cash flow, investors could see $30 million returned or the equivalent of a US$0.20 annualized dividend.

Costs & Margins

As for costs and margins, Wesdome reported Q1'25 AISC of $1,528/oz, up 6% from $1,445/oz from the year-ago period. The increase in AISC was primarily related to the disappointing performance at Kiena, higher sustaining capital spend across the portfolio and higher royalties at Eagle River offset by strong performance at this Ontario mine. Fortunately, the record gold price still resulted in an increase in AISC margins to $1,751/oz (Q2 '24: $888/oz), translating to 53.4% AISC margins or a 1,500+ basis point increase year-over-year.

However, I wouldn't be shocked to see Wesdome's AISC dip back below $1,400/oz in 2026 with better performance out of Kiena, setting up further margin expansion next year assuming gold prices continue to hang out above $3,300/oz. And as noted previously, we should see much better AISC in Q4 '25 as Kiena expects to see its strongest quarter of the year.

The bigger opportunity here, though, is that both mines have considerable excess capacity to leverage their fixed cost base, and Wesdome could see a further improvement in costs medium-term if it were to grow into a 250,000 - 300,000 ounce producer. This is a work in progress, but Wesdome is one of the few names in the sector with the ability to grow organically with modest capex in a low-risk manner, with tapping into excess capacity being one of the more desirable avenues for growth vs. greenfields expansions that are much more expensive, are more complex on a relative basis and are at the mercy of new permits being issued.

Recent Developments

As for recent developments, the most recent was the announcement of Wesdome's Independent Director, Philip Yee, as its new CFO. Philip Yee was previously at Eldorado Gold (EGO) from 2018 to 2024 as CFO. Prior to that, he spent two years as EVP and CFO at Kirkland Lake Gold and three years at Lakeshore Gold as SVP and CFO prior to its sale to Pan American Silver (PAAS). Philip Yee will replace Raj Gill as interim CFO, with the new position set to begin on September 29th.

Eagle River Exploration

As for other developments, Wesdome continues to see exploration success at Eagle River. This included extending the 6 Central Zone by 300 meters to 600 meters down-plunge, with highlight intercepts of 1.6 meters (true width) at 115.9 G/T of gold, 2.0 meters (true width) at 49.1 G/T of gold and 3.2 meters (true width) at 47.0 G/T of gold. Wesdome noted that drilling is closing the gap towards 758-E-500, a high-grade intercept that hit 40.0 G/T of gold over 6.3 meters (true width) and which was excluded from the year-end 2024 resource update.

As it stands, Eagle River's reserves sit at ~487,000 ounces at 12.3 G/T of gold, which supports roughly 5 years of mine life, but the addition of considerable regional upside, the up-plunge potential of the 720 Falcon Zone near surface and the confirmation of the geological model at the 300 F zone as a separate structure from the 300 Zone are certainly very encouraging developments. Combined with considerable land added in the Angus Gold acquisition, including Dorset, and further potential at Mishi/Magnacon with Wesdome planning to test the down plunge extend of Mishi below its open-pit, there's certainly reason to be very optimistic about resource growth and resource conversion going forward.

And, like Kiena, Eagle River is operating at just half of its permitted capacity of 1,200 TPD, suggesting opportunity to push production higher at this mine as well longer-term. Hence, it's not just Kiena that has production upside, though Kiena offers the most leverage from a production upside standpoint given that it's using just one-third of permitted capacity currently at Kiena Deep (will increase to ~60% once Presqu'ile online).

Notably, Wesdome already owns two of the highest-grade gold mines in top-ranked jurisdictions, with Eagle River and Kiena highlighted below relative to other underground projects/mines based on reserves and reserve grades.

Labor

On a negative note, Wesdome wasn't shy about labor being a "major challenge." This is not surprising given the considerable growth by producers in the region like Agnico Eagle (AEM) and others, with Agnico alone looking to grow production by nearly 1.0 million ounces combined over the next several years (DL-UG - ~300,000 ounces, Upper Beaver - ~200,000 ounces, Canadian Malartic Complex - ~400,000 ounces). So, while Wesdome has ambitions to materially expand production, the lack of skilled labor could pose a challenge, especially with competition for labor only heating up in a very favorable gold price environment and in a prolific region.

Valuation

Based on ~154 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$15.90, Wesdome trades at a market cap of ~$2.45 billion and an enterprise value of ~$2.31 billion. This continues to be a very reasonable valuation for a ~200,000 ounce producer with high-grade mines in top-ranked jurisdictions, and a far more palatable valuation than the ~$10 billion for Coeur Mining (CDE) in less favorable jurisdictions with a track record of per share destruction vs. Wesdome's track record of per share growth.

In fact, despite Wesdome sitting at all-time highs, the stock trades at a very reasonable valuation at less than ~10x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates ($3,350/oz gold) and just over 7.6x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates at a $3,600/oz gold price. for a dual-asset producer with organic growth potential.

And if we look out three years and assume Wesdome can grow into a 260,000 ounce producer by leveraging excess mill capacity, Wesdome trades at just ~3.7x FY2028 EV/FCF (flat $3,400/oz gold price in 2026-2028 period), making it one of the more interesting buy-the-dip candidates if we do see a decent pullback.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 9.0x cash flow and 1.2x P/NAV and a 7% discount rate with gold price assumptions of $3,350/oz (2026), $3,000/oz (2027) and $2,700/oz long-term, I see a fair value for the stock of US$17.90. This points to an 11% upside from current levels, but I am looking for at least a 27% discount to fair value for Tier-1 only producers of this scale to ensure a margin of safety. Hence, while I think Wesdome is a solid story with a promising long-term outlook, I don't see a low-risk buying opportunity in the stock yet using my more conservative base case assumptions.

As for the technical picture, Wesdome is in the upper end of its projected support/resistance range, with short-term resistance at US$16.10 and upper support at US$11.70, with the stock extended short-term and vulnerable to a correction. That said, if we were to see a pullback into the lower portion of this range, I would consider starting a new position in the stock.

Summary

Wesdome had a tough H1 '25 at Kiena but should see improved results in the second half and will benefit from record gold prices. Notably, the company is a unique name in the sector given its considerable excess permitted mill capacity, giving it a low-risk and relatively low capex path to organic growth at its two key assets, similar to Agnico Eagle (AEM) and unlike many of its peers relying on new builds to increase production. That said, the stock is still outside of its ideal buy zone and is getting a little extended here above US$16.00/share. Hence, while I think Wesdome makes for a solid buy-the-dip candidate, I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock above US$16.00.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Plug Power: This Market Has No Fear (Technical Analysis, Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Plug Power Inc. (NASDAQ:PLUG) has rallied over 120% in the last six months.

While there have been some positive catalysts for the company, I believe this is symptomatic of a market that is not afraid of risk.

The right ingredients are in place for speculative names like Plug to keep running from here. But how much further can we go?

I lay out some targets based on technical analysis.

Plug Power’s Recent Rally

Plug has squeezed over $100 in the last few months, reigniting interest in this once highly followed stock.

At its peak in 2021, Plug traded at over $70. In the last few years, though, the company has failed to deliver on its promises, and it even had to restate its financials at one point.

However, it seems like the recent earnings may have actually reignited investor interest, at least enough to cause a short squeeze.

In Q2 2025, Plug updated investors on “Project Quantum Leap,” which will focus on margin recovery, operational streamlining, and better cash management.

In the last quarter, revenue grew 21% year-over-year to $174 million, and electrolyzer sales tripled to $45 million.

Gross margins improved from negative 92% a year ago to negative 31%, and management reiterated its target of achieving gross margin neutrality by Q4 and positive EBITDA in 2026.

In terms of the balance sheet, there’s $140 million in cash and $300 million in available debt capacity.

There are also hopes that recent U.S. tax credit legislation could accelerate electrolyzer adoption and strengthen customer economics.

The company is targeting $700 million in 2025 and recently delivered its first 100MW electrolyzer.

Plug Power Stock Performance Is An Indicator Of Market Euphoria

But if you ask me, Plug’s recent rally is more a symptom of market euphoria than any tangible fundamentals.

Of course, there’s been some “positive” news in the last few weeks, but this is a stock that ultimately trades based on momentum.

At the height of the 2021 rally, it reached over $70, but this was mostly led by speculation rather than anything else. As we can see below, the P/S ratio exploded to over 30.

This was a time of ample liquidity, with tight credit yields and investors searching for returns further out on the risk curve.

We’re seeing things play out similarly now as we begin a Fed cutting cycle, and possibly something much more dovish, with President Trump and his administration pushing the Fed in this direction.

  • Small caps have begun to outperform.

  • Chinese stocks are back in fashion.

  • Some Altcoins are beginning to outperform Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

  • And we are seeing short squeezes all over the place.

The Plug rally is simply the latest symptom of a market that is entering a euphoric stage.

This doesn’t mean a top is necessarily close. As I’ve mentioned, the Fed has just begun cutting, and we have an administration adamant on keeping the economy hot.

Plug Power: Technical Analysis

My point to PLUG investors and traders alike is to be very careful moving forward.

In my opinion, looking at catalysts and technical levels is more important for a stock like this than anything else.

Looking at the 4h chart, PLUG is now approaching its first real test, the $3 level, where it topped out weeks ago and also where it tipped out back in July and January.

From a momentum perspective, though, we have now cleared the key EMAs and have a MACD about to flip bullish.

Looking at the weekly chart, we can identify some longer-term support and resistance levels. Below us, the $1.8 level would be key to hold in any retracement to keep the bullish momentum.

In terms of upside, I do think Plug could target the 200-week EMA at $7.2, with room to turn into the 78.6% retracement level at $16.

If we could get back near $2, I’d consider going long, with plans to exit around $7.

Final Thoughts

Personally, I’ve been burned on Plug before, and it’s not a stock I’d ever own long-term. In my opinion, the current rally is more based on speculation and macro dynamics than on any real bullish fundamentals.

But this doesn't mean we can’t profit. I do think this is a stock that has the potential to triple from here as market euphoria continues to increase.

data.title: CVR Partners: Higher Prices And Expanded Production Will Improve The EPS Forecast
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The fundamental analysis of the market situation in the mineral fertilizer sector suggests that demand for these products will increase in 2026. This fact leads me to look for shares of companies that have strong competitive positions in their industry. Among them is the American manufacturer CVR Partners, LP (NYSE:UAN). Its subsidiaries are showing an increase in production capacity utilization against the backdrop of an 18% increase in the average selling price of products. This could increase the company's profits by 34% in 2025. Moreover, there are plans to increase production volumes, which, in parallel with the development of pricing policy, could increase sales of nitrogen fertilizers by +13.76%. The assessment of the fair value of CVR Partners shares allows us to determine their growth potential at +38.3% to $125.57 per share. Therefore, my recommendation is “Buy.”

CVR Partners Company Overview

CVR Partners LP operates in the mineral fertilizer industry, which is primarily driven by the need to ensure global consumer safety. The shortage of agricultural goods caused by various fundamental factors is driving increased demand for chemicals among agricultural producers. Such chemicals are used to increase the yield of agricultural land. The triggers include the war in Ukraine, population growth, disruption of food transport and logistics chains, global warming, and climate change. The current conditions for farming are getting worse, which means that extra fertilizer is needed to make sure crops reach their planned yield. CVR Partners focuses on making nitrogen fertilizers, which include ammonia and urea-ammonium fertilizers.

The company's competitive advantages include plants managed by CVR Partners. The most important subsidiary is Coffeyville Resources Nitrogen Fertilizers, LLC, which produces nitrogen fertilizers at a unique facility. The management says, “It's the only plant like this in North America that uses oil coke gasification in its production process to get hydrogen.” The subsidiaries also include East Dubuque Nitrogen Fertilizers, LLC, which makes nitrogen fertilizers using natural gas.

CVR Partners Financial Profile: Q2 2025 Data Suggests Upward EPS Estimates

The financial stability of CVR Partners is improving, as evidenced by the results of the quarterly report for the first half of 2025. "The second quarter showed an increase in net profit to $39 million, or $3.67 per share (EPS). EBITDA amounted to $67 million, and revenue showed an increase of 26.8%." I believe that the company's financial results will continue to improve. Among the main reasons for this is the forecast for the market conditions in the nitrogen fertilizer industry in 2026. Additionally, the low GAAP P/E ratio (TTM) of 10.91 (compared to the industry median of 24.42) contributes to the positive assessment of its investment attractiveness.

With CVR Partners' profits growing, the company's management has announced a record dividend payment starting in August 2023 ($3.89 per common share). This is a clear signal to investors that its shares are becoming more attractive. Current values are far from those of 5-10 years ago. But due to profit growth, the process of increasing payments may continue. Besides, CVR Partners' average dividend yield is 17.21% over the last 4 years. The industry median is only ~2% by comparison.

The Mineral Fertilizer Market Is Booming

In analyzing CVR Partners' prospects, it is worth noting that overall trends in the global mineral fertilizer market indicate that nutrient consumption is expected to grow to 205 million tons by 2025. This would exceed the previous record. Ammonia production capacity will likely increase by 4% annually from 2023 to 2025 (largely due to Russia and the US, with cheaper mineral fertilizer production costs). The volume of nitrogen consumption will also likely increase to 116 million tons in 2025. This signals an increase in planned production volumes, which means additional profits from the sale of mineral fertilizers by CVR Partners' subsidiaries.

As such, I believe that the nitrogen fertilizer market will be characterized by signs of a boom in the foreseeable future. In addition to the parallel growth in demand for products and their production, we are seeing an increase in prices, which is improving the financial results of manufacturers. Such drivers as the increase in the world's population, a decrease in agricultural land for arable farming, and the desire to increase agricultural efficiency in the Asia-Pacific region and Africa will create conditions for maintaining the previous average annual growth rate of the global nitrogen fertilizer market at 4-6%.

To be sure, the cost of production also affects the price of mineral fertilizers. Natural gas prices account for about 80% of the cost. The current trade policy and macroeconomic situation in the world are creating uncertainty, which may lead to further increases in energy prices. But balanced production capacity (replacing Russian gas with American liquefied natural gas) is making natural gas prices more stable, limiting their potential growth and making it easier to predict nitrogen fertilizer prices.

A previously anticipated energy crisis, which had been forecast since 2022, did not live up to the negative predictions of skeptical analysts. The current price situation on the hydrocarbon market provides a clear picture of the financial planning of nitrogen fertilizer producers' cost estimates. The volatility of natural gas prices has changed significantly from historically high and shocking levels in 2022 to noticeably lower ones. As the natural gas market keeps getting more stable in 2025, we can say that the energy crisis is over. This fact has a positive impact on the nitrogen fertilizer market, keeping up its growth and maintaining the growing demand from agricultural producers.

Geopolitics Creates Conditions for Business Development

The geopolitical backdrop is usually considered a trigger that creates risks and threats for business growth. But with CVR Partners, 2025 will continue to have a positive effect on boosting financial results. This is because the company is under US jurisdiction. CVR Partners has a good starting point and ample access to natural gas orders (while being subject to the same market pressures and volatility as all natural gas users). This supply of resources allows the company to maintain a stable production cycle, fulfilling orders on time and maintaining high production capacity utilization (around 91%). The continued instability in the European and Middle Eastern natural gas markets creates unfavorable conditions for nitrogen fertilizer producers in European countries (which have higher production costs due to natural gas prices and production interruptions) and the Middle East and North Africa (due to temporary disruptions in natural gas supplies to producers, especially in Egypt, which resulted in wasted time as many nitrogen fertilizer plants were idle).

Moreover, the crisis surrounding Ukraine, in which the Trump administration is increasingly dissatisfied with the negotiations with Putin, creates geopolitical risks of continued military conflict. This factor is important, first, because Russian gas is still not fully available to European producers. Previously, a diplomatic solution to the conflict was expected in 2025, which would have resumed natural gas supplies from Russia to European hubs. But this is unlikely to happen before 2026.

For instance, in spring 2025, the Polymarket forecasting website predicted a 78% probability of a diplomatic end to the war by the end of the year. This figure had fallen to 10% by October 1, 2025. As the conflict continues, practical solutions that would allow for negotiations on lifting sanctions on Russia's energy sector and resuming natural gas supplies to EU countries, even through Ukraine's gas transportation system, are being put on hold. In 2021, for example, Russian gas supplies amounted to 41.7 billion cubic meters.

Secondly, it is important to remember that it was the sanctions imposed on Russia in 2022 that led to the partial loss of access to Western markets for the largest producers of nitrogen fertilizers. As an example, we can take PhosAgro, the largest Russian producer of nitrogen fertilizers in the world. With an annual output of 11.8 million tons of mineral fertilizers, the company occupies a leading position in the global industry. From a total volume of 2.6 million tons, this is ammonia production. Considering that in 2024 the global market volume was 114 million tons, PhosAgro has a 2.3% share. A free flow of such a huge volume of mineral fertilizers into the European and American markets could lead to the opposite effect of the sanctions—a decrease in ammonia prices. For CVR Partners, this would mean a decline in profit growth. But as the geopolitical situation stays shaky and the news at the end of September brings some negative surprise, according to President Trump, the positive impact of geopolitics on business development is likely to continue.

The Valuation Shows +13.76% in Sales

Considering the rapid development of the modern mineral fertilizer industry, CVR Partners' management has decided to begin implementing plans to expand production capacity in order to increase ammonia production by 8%. For this purpose, an investment plan worth $55–65 million will be implemented, as high demand is creating reasons for an increase in product sales prices. The limited reserves in CVR Partners' warehouses do not allow for a significant increase in sales in the future without expanding the capacity for ammonia production.

The practical question arises as to how feasible CVR Partners' plans are and what income they can generate in the context of the boom in the mineral fertilizer market. The primary focus of production expansion is ammonia products. Let us assess how this decision will affect potential profits.

In Q2 2025, “a total of 197,000 tons of ammonia were produced, of which 54,000 tons net were available for sale, with the remainder being processed into other fertilizers, including 321,000 tons of urea-ammonia fertilizers.” Also, Q2 2025 saw an increase in the average selling price of ammonia and urea ammonium fertilizers by 14% and 18%, respectively, compared to Q2 2024 prices. The average selling price of mineral fertilizers is now $593 and $317, respectively. This results in CVR Partners selling $32.022 million worth of ammonia and $101.757 million worth of urea and ammonium fertilizers. Total sales of these products amount to $133.779 million.

Let's now look at CVR Partners' financial capabilities. Based on the results of the second quarter of 2025, the company has accumulated a cash balance of $114.4 million. Given that an 8% increase in ammonia production requires an average of $60 million, a 1% increase in production capacity costs $7.5 million. To implement its production expansion plan, the company could spend 52.44% of all its cash on the balance sheet. But if the company uses all the $100+ million available, production could grow by 15.25% instead of 8%. Of course, the company won't take this step right away and will use borrowed funds to increase production capacity. It should be understood that most of the cash is needed for other purposes.

Let us now proceed to the valuation itself. Considering that CVR Partners will spend an average of $60 million on expanding production, the effect of increasing the volume of ammonia produced will be 8% or about 213,000 tons in real terms. According to the company's distribution of ammonia production between ammonia sales and the production of urea-ammonia fertilizers, the ratio is 27% to 73%. The result is that of the 8% increase in production, 2.16% will go to sales of ammonia itself and 5.84% to the production of urea-ammonia fertilizers. As such, ammonia sales will increase from 54,000 tons to 55,166 tons, while urea-ammonia fertilizer sales will increase from 321,000 tons to 339,746 tons.

Considering that over the past 12 months, prices for ammonia and urea-ammonium fertilizers have risen by 14% and 18%, respectively, I think that in the next six months, these increases may continue at the same pace and reach a median of 7% and 9%. On this basis, the selling prices of the products will be $634 and $345, respectively.

It should be noted right away that forecasting ammonia price growth in the second half of 2025 is fraught with difficulties. This is due to the influence of several groups of factors. The greater the number of triggers affecting nitrogen fertilizer pricing, the more difficult it is to predict their price in the foreseeable future. Some of the most important factors include supply in Western markets, where Russian producers still have a minimal presence. Other key factors include changes in inflation, market volatility in natural gas prices, and foreign trade policy, which could lead to additional customs tariffs.

Nevertheless, the consensus is that nitrogen fertilizer prices will continue to grow at the same pace. This is primarily due to a lack of confidence that imports of Russian fertilizers will return to Western mineral fertilizer markets in full by the end of 2025. In addition, inflationary risks remain. Analysts predict that the next US inflation figures will probably remain at the previous level of +2.9%. The inflationary trend is driving up food prices. It is directly linked to rising prices for agricultural products, whose producers are consumers of nitrogen fertilizers.

The additional introduction of tariffs by the European Union on imports of mineral fertilizers from Russia should be considered an additional factor. The cost of the additional tariff for Russian ammonia will be $40-45 per ton of fertilizer. These updates happened in July 2025 and only affected the price of nitrogen fertilizers starting in the third quarter. Among other things, pushing up ammonia prices is growing demand from farmers in India and Brazil.

As such, CVR Partners' sales of ammonia and urea-ammonia fertilizers, after implementing plans to expand production by 8%, will amount to $34.975 million and $117.212 million. Total sales will amount to $152.187 million. A final increase in sales from $133.779 million to $152.187 million will represent a growth of +13.76%.

Risks to the Investment Thesis

Studying CVR Partners' financial profile and business strategy has led me to a positive assessment of the investment attractiveness of their shares. Meanwhile, the growth in chemical sales prices will most likely continue to be supported by market conditions in the mineral fertilizer sector. Nevertheless, CVR Partners' business model is subject to a number of risks that could affect the further growth of its share price.

Although CVR Partners' investment attractiveness is viewed positively, its business model is subject to a number of risks. Geopolitical factors pose the most significant risks, which are currently creating conditions for nitrogen fertilizer prices to rise. The problem of ensuring the security of global supply chains is of secondary importance for CVR Partners, as production is resource-supported by domestic US gas and the main sales of products are in North America. At the same time, however, a significant improvement in the geopolitical risk situation could have the opposite effect, leading to a decline in nitrogen fertilizer prices (as the market will see an increase in the supply of mineral fertilizers from producers, primarily players such as PhosAgro). In addition, the continued imposition of sanctions on Russian companies creates an additional unpredictable factor that will significantly affect pricing in the near term.

Further factors negatively impacting CVR Partners' profit growth prospects include potential increases in natural gas prices and changes in electricity costs in the US states. But the impact of these problems isn't new, and it always seems more serious in winter. U.S. spot natural gas prices over the past couple of years are shown in the figure below. They represent stable price dynamics. In the event of a change in weather conditions and a reorientation of US liquefied gas producers towards the European market, however, conditions may be created for price increases within the US.

Stock Forecast and Targets

I used a DCF model for my valuation. It will yield comparative results, which allow us to determine the level of investment attractiveness. Instead of FCFF, I will use the distributable cash flow indicator to assess the fair value of CVR Partners.

For the projection part of the DCF model, I have chosen the nearest 5-year period from 2025 to 2029. The key valuation tool is the discount rate. The WACC rate used in my calculations is approximately 7.9%. CVR Partners' expected profit growth in the forecast period will range from 34% in 2025 to 4% in 2029. This rapid profit growth in 2025 is due to the results of the first half of the year. Q1 EBITDA was $53 million, and Q2 EBITDA was $67 million. This brings CVR Partners' EBITDA for the first half of 2025 to $120 million. Assuming that the second half of 2025 maintains the same profit growth rate, total EBITDA will be $240 million. Continued profit growth is driven by economic conditions in the mineral fertilizer market. The company forecasts profit growth for the entire mineral fertilizer sector over the next three years at 21.2%. Conclusion
