The change in valuation may be influenced by both risks and additional incentives. I believe that my projected EBITDA growth to 2029 of $290 million is modest. Considering that EBITDA was $402 million in 2022, its return to previous levels will represent a profit growth of +124.58%. Therefore, after the publication of the financial statements for the third quarter of 2025, the fair value estimate of CVR Partners may be revised upwards. Meanwhile, the planned expansion of production could increase sales of nitrogen fertilizers by +13.76%. Considering the company's high solvency and financial stability, the expected growth in demand in the global market will generate renewed ideas about expanding production capacity at its plants.

data.title: Tesla: Take Profits Before The Correction, Sales Trends Are Worsening (Downgrade)
data.description:
Despite all the negative headlines that cloud the market: weak jobs data, stretched valuations, and declining consumer spending: large-cap growth stocks have continued to surge upward, including those that initially shouldered a big correction this year. Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) has been a major beneficiary: its stock has swung widely in a band between $250 to $500 this year, and now after CEO Elon Musk's departure from the Trump administration, Tesla is back into positive territory with a ~20% gain this year.

The question for investors now is: with Tesla back in the trillion-dollar club, do we keep holding or lock in gains?

I'll cut to the chase here: at this juncture, I'm more of an opportunistic trader versus a long-term investor Tesla. Back in June, when Tesla was still trading in the low $300s, I recommended a Buy on the stock: but now having gained ~50% on my position, I'm more than happy to lock in gains and am downgrading Tesla to a Sell rating. Worry not: there will be another opportunity to enter into Tesla soon, and at a much lower price.

Pay attention to weak sales data, especially with high expectations ahead of tax credit expiry

Part of what has constantly fueled the rally in Tesla is the hope that it is more than just a car company, but an innovator at the forefront of robotics, automation, robotaxis, and a host of other futuristic inventions.

The reality, however, is that at least in the next 3-5 year timeframe, Tesla is a car company: and car sales are worsening.

In Q2, deliveries of total Tesla vehicles declined -13% y/y to 384.1k units. Model 3/Y units declined -12% y/y, in line with the decay in Q1, while the "other models" (S and X) declined -52% y/y to just 10.4k deliveries, much worse than a -24% y/y decline in Q1.

Recently in October, Tesla also reported its vehicle sales ahead of its formal earnings print, as usual. The deliveries for the September quarter are shown below:

Total deliveries of 497.1k for Q3 rose 12% y/y, while 3/Y deliveries grew 8% y/y. Other models continued to decline at a sharp -39% y/y pace.

At face value, this looks like a return to growth for Tesla. But this was largely due to a pull-in of last-time buy orders due to the expiration of the $7,500 EV tax credit on September 30. Even with this catalyst in place, Tesla could only achieve single-digit growth in 3/Y sales. When we consider a rolling two-quarter sales trend across Q2 and Q3, 3/Y deliveries are still down -1% despite the Q3 EV-induced sales bump.

We can and should put Model 3/Y weakness off to one side: there's likely a swath of buyers holding out for the upcoming rumored "Model Q", which was initially promised as a lower-cost, budget version of both the Models 3 and Y. Tesla insists that this model is still coming, but has been very sparse on details. CFO Vaibhav Taneja noted on the Q2 earnings call that Tesla has begun production on the new models in the first half of FY25, but that the production ramp will happen in Q3, which may delay the eventual release.

The -39% Q3 decline in the high-end S and X vehicles, however, to me signals a reflection of Tesla's eroding brand equity. Tesla is no longer the premium, cool car around the block: the accessibility of Model 3 and Y has made it a relatively common ride in the U.S. and around the world. Add on top of that the complications from Elon Musk's political reputation and the fact that driving a Tesla may signal a certain political opinion, and I think we're set up for an erosion in Tesla's brand prestige, which is clearly signaled in the decline of S and X deliveries - which in my view are a different market segment and face little cannibalization from the upcoming lower-cost launch.

We think that 3/Y and its next-gen successor will continue to face pressure with the expiration of the $7,500 tax credit, especially with gas prices falling all year amid an oversupply of crude oil. The economics of an EV are simply no longer as attractive. And no matter how much cheaper the next-gen Model 3 and Y cars are, it's unlikely to be cheaper than today's models with the $7,500 credit factored in (though to be fair, higher-income taxpayers are excluded from taking this credit, and so the new vehicles will be cheaper on a net basis). In short, we should expect Tesla's recent weak sales trends to continue.

Profitability slide, with added risk from regulatory credits ending

As longtime Tesla investors are aware, one of the company's most consistent and highest-margin revenue streams has been selling its regulatory emissions credits to other automakers, which is included in the company's "services and other revenue" line.

Taneja warned that this windfall will end on the Q2 earnings call:

The One Big Bill has a lot of changes that would affect our business in the near term. The first among those changes is the repeal of the IRA EV credit of $7,500 by the end of this quarter. Given the abrupt change, we have limited supply of vehicles in the U.S. this quarter as we have already with the lead times to order parts to build cars. We have rolled out all our planned incentives already and we'll start paring them back as we start to sell. If you are in the U.S. and looking to buy a car, place your order now as we may not be able to guarantee delivery orders placed in the later part of August and beyond.

The bill also made changes to certain emission standards by reducing the amount of penalty to zero. This, in turn, will have an impact on the new sales of regulatory credits to other OEMs and, in turn, will lead to lower revenue. While we now plan our business around such sales, it will nonetheless impact our total revenues going forward."

As shown in the chart below, regulatory credits amounted to $439 million of revenue in Q2, and $1.03 billion in the first half of FY25, or roughly 2% of revenue.

But this revenue stream is pure profit. The loss of these credits, stacked on top of brand-new tariff burdens (the company notes that tariff costs rose sequentially by $300 million in Q2, and that the environment remains "unpredictable") complicates matters even further.

In Q2, Tesla's gross margins fell -71bps y/y to 17.2%. Q3 is likely to see even sharper margin pressure as regulatory credit sales dwindle and as the company shoulders a full quarter of tariff burdens, versus only a partial quarter in Q2.

And as shown above, we note that Tesla's operating margin slid -219bps y/y to 4.1%, and adjusted EBITDA fell -7% y/y on a nominal basis to $3.40 billion. The only potential upside here is the fact that Tesla has applied for regulatory approval to open more robotaxi routes. The company's autopilot director, Ashok Elluswamy, noted as follows on the Q&A portion of the Q2 earnings call:

The next thing to expand would be in the San Francisco Bay Area. We are working with the government to get approval here and, in the meanwhile, launch the service without the person in the driver seat just to expedite and while we wait for regulatory approval. We are also testing a lot of other cities in the U.S., including Florida, Nevada, et cetera."

Faster adoption of robotaxi services, plus an infusion of new sales from the lower-cost models, are the only near-term upside catalysts I can see in Tesla stock, and even these will be hard-pressed to reverse recent profit declines.

Valuation and key takeaways

Unfortunately, with Tesla's recent resurgence, the stock is back to trading at very outlandish valuation multiples. As shown below, the stock has now soared to a >100x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple:

This is unsupportable when adjusted EBITDA is actually in decline, driven by gross margin weakness, regulatory changes that will pin down sales on multiple fronts, and potential erosion on Tesla's brand appeal: and with very few levers to reverse that decline. Tesla is likely still years away from achieving meaningful scale with Robotaxi, as well as rolling out other initiatives like Optimus and automation. Above $450 (or for that matter, anywhere in this neighborhood), Tesla is a clear Sell.

data.title: Lockheed Martin: Shift In Segment Concentration Could Address Fundamental Red Flags
data.description:
Sentiment around Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT) has been mixed during 2025, with the second-quarter announcement in July creating an instant panic within the investor base. Although there has been a resurgence in share price since then, the aerospace & defense giant is in need of strategic realignment in terms of its operating segments. Such realignment is needed after some negative developments in recent times. Some notable ones include interest by the U.S. government to acquire a stake in the business, losing bids on major projects to rival companies, and taking massive losses & charges on some of their ongoing programs. Beyond that, the flagship F-35 program has also gone out of favor, as the Department of Defense (DoD) has expressed concerns over the associated costs and delays related to TR-3 update.

In this analysis, I will present my bearish stance on LMT despite its cheaper valuations relative to peers. I will support my argument by throwing light on the structural issues mentioned above and related risks. I will also evaluate the company’s financial standing, key developments during the third quarter, and future prospects. So let’s begin!

Business Segments

Lockheed Martin operates through 4 business segments. As can be seen in the graph presented above, Aerospace is the largest contributor to LMT’s revenues, with a 40% contribution during the first half of 2025. This segment runs various large-scale programs, including F-35 Lightning II, C-130 Hercules, and F-16 Falcon. The importance of the aerospace segment can be emphasized through LMT’s flagship F-35 program, which contributed 30% to the company’s overall revenues in 2024.

The Missiles and Fire Control (MFC) segment develops advanced equipment and systems for manned and unmanned combat. Apart from serving the U.S. military forces and its allies, this segment also caters to the global civil nuclear power industry.

Rotary and Mission Systems (RMS) is the second largest business segment in terms of revenue contribution. Accounting for almost a quarter of the LMT’s topline, this segment operates circa 900 programs in more than 100 countries across the globe. Amongst many of its unique capabilities are the sensor and radar systems covering both land and sea.

Finally, the Space segment gives a futuristic edge to the broader business through expertise in communication, exploration, and security.

The Flagship F-35 Program

The second quarter involved important developments that raised investor uncertainty concerning the company’s F-35 program. For years, this program held an integral position within the global combat jet market, with the F-35 Lightning II being dubbed as the most lethal fifth-generation strike fighter in the world. However, the jet’s high lifetime costs, delays on technology upgrades, and issues linked with delivery and ongoing maintenance have made it a less viable option compared to more advanced alternatives.

There have been continued delays on the original timeline for the Technology Refresh 3 (TR-3), which is a software and hardware upgrade critical for the modernization of Block 4. These upgrades were originally scheduled to be finalized by April 2023, but so far, LMT has performed upgrades limited only to training use. As per the revised timeline, a series of key tests for full combat certifications is not likely to start before 2026. As per the annual Operation Test and Evaluation report, released earlier this year, the program “has shown no improvement in meeting schedule and performance timelines for developing and testing software designed to address deficiencies and add new capabilities.”

Given such uncertainties, the U.S. Department of Defense decided to curb its request for F-35 jets, both for the Air Force and the Navy. The announcement came in June and revealed that the procurement for the Air Force had been cut down by half compared to 48 jets in 2024. For the Naval forces, the request was made for only 12 jets compared to the budgeted 17. Soon after this announcement, President Trump indicated further cuts in F-35 purchases in 2026, as he wants to shift focus towards the F-47 Next Generation Air Dominance (NGAD) and Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). What is also important to note is that these developments relating to the F-35 came at the back of LMT losing the bid to Boeing (NYSE:BA) for the NGAD Program earlier this year. President Trump opted for Boeing in the allocation of a $20 billion contract for the development of the sixth-generation fighter jet, which will replace LMT’s F-22 Raptor.

Financials

Strong demand has led to revenue growth within all 4 business segments, with sales of $71.04 billion recorded in 2024. This represents a 5-year CAGR of 19% since 2019, when the total revenues were $59.81 billion. LMT registered sales of $36 billion during the first six months of 2025, compared to $35.1 billion during the same period in 2024. Therefore, the LMT management reaffirmed their sales guidance for 2025, forecasted above $74 billion.

Profitability margins have seen a dip since 2023, especially the EBITDA and net margin figures. The underlying reasons are linked with higher-than-anticipated project costs on long-term fixed-cost defense contracts. Although it is not uncommon for companies like Lockheed Martin to incur slightly higher costs on such projects, in recent periods these differences have become larger. This has also resulted in large losses on the books related to LMT’s ongoing projects, which will be discussed in further detail.

LMT’s second-quarter earnings announcement back in July sparked a panic amongst the investors. Revenues for the quarter stood at $18.2 billion, marginally missing the analysts expectations. The company did post a non-GAAP EPS of $7.29, which was an outperformance relative to the Wall Street forecasts. However, this did not induce a positive sentiment in the market because on a GAAP basis, EPS was recorded at $1.46 per share. This meant a whopping 80% decline compared to the same period last year. Such a monumental difference of $5.83 between the two EPS calculations actually relates to pre-tax losses and additional charges of around $1.8 billion on some of the ongoing programs, which are discussed later in the article. The overall demand backlog by the end of the second quarter was $166.5 billion, slightly lower than $173 billion in the previous quarter. This decline can be attributed to uncertainties linked with the F-35 program, which is expected to finish in 2025 with up to 190 deliveries.

Taking $1.8 Billion Hit in Q2: One-Off or Recurring?

During the Q2 earnings call, CEO Jim Taiclet addressed the $1.8 billion charges that the company recorded during the second quarter. He gave assurance regarding the future by saying that such a one-time hit to de-risk these legacy programs was taken after thorough deliberations with the company's CFO. Investors reacted positively to such a cleanup, as the LMT share price rallied after the announcement. For context, the following table shows the breakdown of all losses & charges that were recorded during the second quarter.

Shifting focus back on the statements made by the CEO, one needs to look beyond the second quarter results and see if such charges and losses indicate some form of a recurring pattern in recent history. During 2024 as well, LMT recorded $2 billion losses on some of its classified programs, including a $1.4 billion charge during the final quarter on one of the MFC segment programs. This brings the total amount of losses over a course of 6 quarters to $3.8 billion, of which a larger chunk was incurred during the previous 3 quarters. This points towards an increasing skepticism among the investors lately, and also calls for a more cautious approach towards the LMT stock. The final half of 2025 will project a clearer picture of the continuation of such losses.

Cash Flow Deterioration & Payouts Sustainability

Beyond the accounting profitability metrics, LMT’s cash flow position also deteriorated during the second quarter. This was more alarming for a sector heavyweight like LMT, which has always attracted interest from investors because of its strong cash flow position. In fact, it is due to such cash flows that LMT is now just 3 years shy of becoming a dividend aristocrat, having exhibited annual dividend growth for 22 consecutive years.

During the quarter, the company used up more cash than it could generate, resulting in a negative $150 million in free cash flow. Compare this to $1.51 billion during the same period in 2024, and you get an idea of how much deterioration occurred. Management has attributed this to a high amount of cash tied up in inventory and receivables, as well as the amounts withheld by the U.S. government on account of F-35 program delays.

How much the current situation will affect LMT’s solid payout history is something to be seen in the near future. Both the dividend payout ratio of 93% and the FCF yield-to-dividend yield ratio are well below the sector medians. Hence, it won’t be surprising to see a dividend cut in the foreseeable future.

Regarding dividend cuts, another factor that should not be ignored by LMT shareholders and potential investors is the recent interest shown by the Pentagon in acquiring stakes in major defense industry players. Lockheed Martin is one of the targets, given their massive reliance on the U.S. government for high-value contracts. Many industry experts have expressed their concerns about such planned moves by the Pentagon. They fear this might lead to an innovation halt within the industry, along with unfair practices in allocating large contracts. For shareholders, there is an added risk of politically motivated decision-making that might outrank the shareholder interests.

For a moment, let’s assume that the U.S. Government proceeds to buy a stake in LMT. On one end, LMT could benefit by getting large contracts from the government, which will have voting rights. But on the flip side, the government might be adamant to continue paying hefty dividends to shareholders. One could look at it as a form of state capitalism, where long-term strategic interests of the country are prioritized ahead of shareholder gains. The government might also be more aligned towards funding the company’s large-scale projects organically, by retaining profits. Although it does not sound bad at all for common shareholders, the underlying motivations behind such retention have nothing to do with them.

Altering Revenue Mix for Growth Opportunities

Earlier in this article, I gave a breakdown of revenue contribution from LMT’s four business segments. Aerospace is the biggest driver of topline, generating approximately 40% of the total sales. At the same time, this segment was also the largest contributor to $3.8 billion in project losses and charges recorded during 2024 and 2025. Relate that to the graph presented above, and one can clearly see the segment’s ongoing woes resulting in negative operating margins during the second quarter. With looming uncertainty on the TR-3 upgrades and cost overruns on some of the underlying programs, there is a good chance that struggles for this segment will continue in the near term. There is no doubt that the aerospace segment is currently operating some integral programs that will continue to have a decent demand in the future. But it is unlikely that this segment and the underlying programs will help LMT capitalize on evolving trends within the global defense industry.

Recall from earlier in the article two of the segments, i.e., Missiles & Fire Control (MFC) and Space, contribute around 35-37% to the topline. And this is exactly where the opportunity lies for LMT. The revenue mix needs to be tilted more towards these two segments in light of futuristic developments in the global defense industry. For instance, LMT is currently a major contractor for the U.S. Space Force’s Space-Based Infrared System (SBIRS) program. The purpose of this program, which falls under the company’s Space segment, is to detect threats through satellites that have infrared detectors. In my personal opinion, LMT’s exposure to SBIRS could be a stepping stone to becoming a vital player within the futuristic Golden Dome project. The Golden Dome is a multi-layered missile shield architecture that is being compared with Israel’s Iron Dome. It will use satellite-based sensors and interceptors to detect missile threats from anywhere in the world. Beyond the Golden Dome, this segment could also benefit from the monumental growth expected in the global space economy over the next decade. As perMcKinsey forecasts earlier this year, the space segment will offer a $1.8 trillion opportunity for global economic growth by 2035.

Similarly, various programs being run under the MFC segment could pave the way for LMT to keep up with futuristic trends in defense shielding. Through programs like Next Gen Interceptor (NGI) and the Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) missile system, the MFC segment could prove to be a bedrock in the multi-decade Golden Dome program. The segment was recently awarded a $233 million firm-fixed-price contract with the U.S. Naval Forces. It involves the delivery of 59 infrared receivers, 20 inertial measurement units, and 45 Processors for the U.S. Navy and the Air National Guard (ANG). In the latest development relevant to the MFC segment, the U.S. State Department approved a $1.75 billion sale of M142 High Mobility Artillery Rocket Systems (HIMARS) to Canada, with LMT being the primary contractor. Prior to that, the sale of an additional 48 units of M142 HIMARS to Australia was also sanctioned. This will result in sale proceeds of $705 million, and it follows the delivery of 42 units back in March 2025.

Valuation

The above chart points to a drop in share price during a single trading day, right after LMT announced the second quarter results. But the market sentiment has picked up since the instant panic witnessed on July 22nd, 2025, that saw the share price go down by almost 11% by the close of day. It is a good possibility that investors overbought on the idea that LMT management made deliberate efforts to take one-time hits on their projects to actually pave the way for future growth. But as I have already discussed above, these losses might not be one-time, and investors should not rule out the possibility of more such hits in the near future.

Despite a 22% jump in LMT share price since 22nd July, the stock has still yielded relatively lower on a YTD basis. Compared to broader S&P 500 returns of around 14%, LMT has returned just 3.52% during the first 10 months of 2025. Comparison with listed peers such as Boeing (NYSE:BA), General Dynamics (NYSE:GD), and Northrop Grumman (NYSE:NOC) tells a similar tale. Looking at the YTD returns of 30.65% for GD, 30.19% for NOC, and 25.58% for BA, one could strongly argue that LMT is trading at a very attractive discount to its peers. However, when looking at the valuation multiples, the opinion certainly changes.

LMT’s valuation multiples are enough to convince a fundamentals-driven investor to remain bearish on the stock. The most alarming ones are the price-to-book and price-to-cash-flow metrics. LMT is currently trading at a forward P/B ratio of 22.70, which is 587.31% higher than the sector median. This reflects an enormous overvaluation caused by a decline in the company’s book value per share due to the project losses recorded in recent quarters. Similarly, the TTM price-to-cash flow ratio of 22.98 also shows a 56% overvaluation relative to the sector median.

Risks

Challenges around the integration of the TR-3 update for the F-35 program might lead to further delays on the scheduled timeline. Despite the delivery of non-combat-ready jets, there is a lack of clarity on a complete upgrade that forms the basis for futuristic Block 4 capabilities. There are evident financial implications of such lags, as the costs of completion for these upgrades will mount.

As we highlighted above, LMT is in need of shuffling up its revenue mix and allocating more focus towards MFC and Space segments. Comparing the earlier-mentioned contracts awarded to these two segments with recent developments in the Aerospace and RMS divisions, it becomes evident that LMT management does not seem very ambitious to alter its revenue mix very soon. Take the example of the Rotary and Mission Systems (RMS) segment, which is the second largest contributor. LMT’s subsidiary, Sikorsky, which is a part of the RMS segment, won a $10.85 billion helicopter contract from the U.S. Navy last week. The magnitude of such deals for the RSM and Aerospace segments overshadows the other two areas, which is something that needs to change. Otherwise, the company will continue to face revenue concentration risk.

Finally, there is a risk of a major correction in the LMT stock given its inflated valuation multiples and the possibility of dividend cuts amid a cash crunch. Any such dividend cuts after an impressive track record of payouts will put off investors who might opt to liquidate their holdings in LMT.

Final Word

A section within the market might be tempted by LMT’s attractive valuations, but one should never ignore business fundamentals. Moreover, Seeking Alpha also currently has assigned a Hold rating to LMT with a 2.71 Quant Ratings score. Based on my own analysis, where I highlighted the underlying challenges for management both in the near and long term, I back this rating. I will closely monitor how certain fundamental, industrial, and political factors evolve in the coming quarters before investing in LMT. Prior to that, my stance is Hold.

data.title: Taming Style Rotation Whiplash
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The past 18 months have delivered a masterclass in market whiplash. The growth style of U.S. equities dominated 2023 and 2024 as artificial intelligence (AI)-fueled optimism and mega-cap leadership drove strong returns, to the point of coining the term ‘Magnificent 7'1 to describe seven large companies. To make it crystal clear:

  • The S&P 500 Growth Index returned nearly twice the average annual return, 33.07% compared to 17.18%, of the S&P 500 Value Index over these two years.2

In early 2025, however, the pendulum swung violently in the opposite direction when tariff shocks and policy uncertainty triggered a broad sell-off. For a moment, value exposures looked poised for resurgence. Yet within weeks, markets lurched back to risk-on mode, restoring growth's leadership almost as quickly as it had been questioned. To put numbers to it3:

  • We all remember the shock of the initial tariff announcements in April 2025. From January 1, 2025, to April 8, 2025, the S&P 500 Value Index returned -11.49%, which was markedly better than the S&P 500 Growth Index's return of -18.20%.
  • However, extending to the year-to-date 2025 period as of September 12, 2025, we see the S&P 500 Growth Index up 17.68%, while the S&P 500 Value Index was up 7.83%.

This back-and-forth has left investors grappling with a difficult question: should they chase whichever style regime seems ascendant or attempt to forecast the next rotation? Style leadership can flip on macroeconomic headlines as much as on fundamentals, and attempts to time the turns often leave portfolios exposed at precisely the wrong moment. The April 2025 episode was instructive: investors who leaned entirely into either growth or value found themselves on the wrong side of at least part of the cycle.

That is why a blended approach, anchored in both quality growth and resilient value, has gained importance. At WisdomTree, for those seeking a core U.S. equity strategy, we have:

  • WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund (QGRW): QGRW is designed to track the total return performance, after fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index. The strategy focuses on large-cap U.S. equities that have proved capable of delivering strong return on equity, return on assets and earnings growth metrics.
  • WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV): WTV seeks income and capital appreciation by investing primarily in U.S. equity securities that provide a high total shareholder yield with favorable relative quality characteristics.

With a 50/50 blend of these strategies, investors can construct a core that aims to sidestep the need for perfect timing. Growth holdings capture the structural forces reshaping the economy as well as AI adoption, digital transformation and earnings momentum. At the same time, value holdings bring ballast through shareholder yield, cash flow strength and cyclical participation. Together, they create a portfolio that can help absorb rotation shocks without being defined by them.

As of September 2025, the lesson is clear: markets are rewarding adaptability, not purity. Concentrated bets on a single style may still deliver bursts of outperformance, but they come with stomach-churning volatility and frequent reversals. A core built on the complementary strengths of QGRW and WTV seeks to offer something different: a disciplined way to harness innovation while maintaining resilience through fundamentals. In an era of style rotation whiplash, balance is not a compromise, it is a strategy for durability.

Figure 1: Standardized Performance

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 9/8/25 with returns as of 6/30/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. In the case of WTV, the Fund's objective changed effective 12/18/17. Prior to 12/18/17, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the relevant ticker: QGRW, WTV.

  • Consistent edge: Across nearly every horizon, month-to-date, quarter-to-date, one year and the longest common period, the QGRW/WTV blend either slightly outpaces or keeps pace with the S&P 500 Index, showing it doesn't need a perfect market environment to stay competitive.
  • Compounding outperformance: 2025 has felt like a unique period with the start of the Trump Administration 2.0 and the veritable firehose of policy that has come with it. It's notable that extending beyond 2025 in the one-year and longest common period time frames led to a greater difference in performance, in both cases favoring the QGRW/WTV blend.

Figure 2: The Balance that Beat the Benchmark

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 9/14/25 with returns as of 9/12/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. The Longest Common Period is constrained by the inception of QGRW. In the case of WTV, the Fund's objective changed effective 12/18/17. Prior to 12/18/17, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the relevant ticker: QGRW, WTV.

Judging Consistency: Rolling Period

Figure 3, showing one-year rolling returns, tells a story of consistency with nuance: the 50/50 allocation to QGRW and WTV (blue line) has generally tracked the S&P 500 (black line) closely, but with a modest return advantage during most periods. Both series exhibit the same cyclical rhythm, peaking near 40%–45% rolling returns in late 2024, then dipping sharply into early 2025 before recovering steadily into mid-2025. The allocation's persistent edge, even through drawdowns, underscores how blending quality growth and value tilts can smooth performance and maintain a consistency advantage relative to the market benchmark.

Figure 3: Rolling 1-Year Returns: Consistency with an Edge over the S&P 500 Index

Sources: WisdomTree, Morningstar, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 9/14/25 with returns as of 7/31/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. The Longest Common Period is constrained by the inception of QGRW. In the case of WTV, the Fund's objective changed effective 12/18/17. Prior to 12/18/17, Fund performance reflects the investment objective of the Fund when it tracked the performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performance, click the relevant ticker: QGRW, WTV.

Close Enough to Comfort, Different Enough to Matter

The allocation's sector and stock-level exposures highlight a key point: while there are notable differences compared to the S&P 500 Index, they stop short of being outsized bets. The 50/50 blend of QGRW and WTV, as seen in figure 4a, leans slightly less into Information Technology overall, with more balanced representation in areas like Consumer Discretionary and Financials. At the stock level, as seen in figure 4b, this translates into lighter weights in mega-cap names like Nvidia and Microsoft, though those companies remain core holdings. These distinctions show a conscious effort to diversify rather than concentrate risk, while still keeping the portfolio's largest drivers aligned with the benchmark.

For investors, this positioning offers familiarity without mimicry. Many market participants critique the S&P 500 for its concentration in a handful of technology giants, but they're also hesitant to deviate too far from it, since moving drastically away can trigger discomfort or tracking error concerns. The QGRW/WTV blend strikes a middle ground: recognizable sector and stock exposures that closely resemble the benchmark, but with subtle tilts that emphasize quality growth and value factors. In other words, the portfolio is differentiated enough to add potential value, yet not so different that it risks alienating benchmark-sensitive investors.

Figure 4a: Balanced Sector Positioning Relative to the Benchmark

Figure 4b: Top 10 Holdings: Similar Foundations, Subtly Different Weights

Conclusion: Balanced Positioning for Investors Who Value both Alignment and Edge

In conclusion, the 50/50 blend of QGRW and WTV delivers what many investors seek but rarely find: alignment with the comfort of the S&P 500 benchmark, paired with thoughtful, incremental differentiation. The sector and stock-level exposures show familiar leadership, anchored by the same mega-cap names, but with subtle adjustments that reduce concentration risk without introducing undue tracking error. Rolling returns underscore this balance, demonstrating a pattern of consistency with a modest performance edge. For investors, the result is a portfolio that potentially feels close enough to the benchmark to inspire confidence, yet different enough to matter in driving long-term outcomes.

1 Refers to Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia and Tesla.

2 Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, with data from 1/1/23–12/31/24.

3 Sources for bullets: WisdomTree, FactSet, for specified periods.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

WTV: Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund’s investment process is expected to be heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended.

QGRW: Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. The Fund is non-diversified and, as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit. The Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets and the Index may not perform as intended.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Phoenix Education Partners Prices IPO: Deep Discount Versus Peers
data.description:
Phoenix Education Partners, Inc. (PXED), the parent company of University of Phoenix (UoP), issued an amended S1 filing on 9/30 that provided pricing of the upcoming IPO slated to occur on October 8 and provided more details about the company’s capital structure. I published an article on Seeking Alpha earlier in the month lauding management’s accomplishments in improving student outcomes and promised to provide further insights into the IPO once they priced it. I was a bit taken back with what I read in the filing.

PXED significantly underpriced the offering relative to publicly traded comps.

Arguably closer to a fire-sale valuation than a traditional IPO discount.

I can only speculate as to why that's the case.

Underpricing an IPO to boost demand and encourage new investors to take risks is a familiar concept. You can read about it here and here. What's fascinating about this IPO is the magnitude of the valuation discount. It's been documented that IPOs usually price at a 10-15% discount to incentivize investors to buy. In this case, there is a 50% discount.

In my opinion, this stock may pop on its IPO day, as the stock trades at a comparable valuation to its peers. The question is the speed with which it pops. Retail investors who are nimble may be able to generate some alpha in the early hours of the IPO day. It's conceivable that the stock doesn't fully appreciate on par with its peers. If that's the case, then there is an investment thesis that may take longer to realize.

The Deep Valuation Discount Relative to Peers

Above, I created a comp table for PXED relative to its publicly traded for-profit higher ed peers.

Operationally, PXED provides adult learners with online instruction. It has been repositioned as a career-focused university with an emphasis on B2B corporate partnerships. It is most close in operations to Strategic Education (STRA), where I initiated a position in September, and Perdoceo (PRDO) in business model and risk profile. These two companies operate online universities, although STRA does have international assets in Australia/New Zealand.

Adtalem (ATGE) operates in the higher ed space but isn't exactly a good comp, as the company owns Chamberlain University, the largest nursing school in the country. Given the nursing shortage in the US, Chamberlain, and thus ATGE, is a bit more recession resistant than the other providers. More importantly, nursing schools are notoriously difficult to scale, given, among other things, the scarcity of essential resources like externships and preceptors. This explains its high valuation relative to its peers. This is the best name to own in the group.

American Public (APEI), through its American Military University, has a strong brand in the military and is one of the largest institutions serving the military. It also has a presence in nursing through its Rasmussen University. So while in higher ed, not the best of comps.

PXED could, in my opinion, trade at an average of STRA and PRDO, essentially at a 9x EV/TTM EBITDA. Best-case scenario, it trades at an average of these four stocks or 11x EV/TTM EBITDA.

The underwriters have priced it at 3.7x EV/TTM EBITDA.

Here's my math: The IPO price midpoint is $32/share. They have no debt as of August 31, 2025, and have cash & equivalents of $227M. With 36M basic shares (not diluted), that gets them a $1.1B market cap and an enterprise value of around $900M. The math is not exactly correct because I don't have weighted average diluted shares, but it's close enough. If PXED were to re-rate to peer averages:

- At 9x (STRA/PRDO average), it could trade close to $65/share.

- At 11x (average of STRA/PRDO/APEI/ATGE), it could trade close to $80/share.

IPO Risk

Investors overall should be aware that there are risks in engaging in any IPO. The price might be quite volatile on the IPO day, so investors should be careful in timing their buying and exits.

There are some risks from the S-1 that are worthy of understanding before taking a position.

First off, the private equity sponsors Apollo and Vistria will collectively retain ~70% of the voting power after the IPO. They are only selling 4.25M shares in the IPO (the company itself is not selling shares). These investors are likely to seek future secondary offerings at a market-aligned valuation once PXED establishes a track record. Recall that the private equity sponsors for years wanted to sell the university to a state school, most recently the University of Idaho, but the deal fell through. The public offering is a backup plan. Further selling may establish pressure on shares. As well, investors need to take into account that they effectively have no voting rights.

Secondly, per the S-1 filing, "We expect that purchasers of our common stock in this offering will experience an immediate dilution of $25.46 per share, representing the difference between our as-adjusted net tangible book value per share and the initial public offering price. This dilution is due in large part to earlier investors having paid substantially less than the initial public offering price when they purchased their shares." What this means is that the IPO price is much higher than the company's book value on the balance sheet. This is typical for asset-light service businesses that are valued on earnings rather than book value. What you need to know is that there isn't a valuation backstop regarding assets on the balance sheet.

Third, the S-1 notes that after the IPO, the company will still have about 1.46 billion shares authorized and not yet issued. This means that more shares could be sold in the future that could put downward pressure on the stock.

These risks are typical of any PE-backed IPO for an asset-light services company. Please consider these risks before taking a position in the stock.

Regulatory Risks Muted Under Trump Administration

A common concern when investing in for-profit higher ed is potential regulatory challenges. It's difficult enough to manage a business, but in this industry, managers need to take into account potential actions by the government, particularly that these schools are funded with government loans and grants. In my opinion, regulatory issues may not emerge under the Trump Administration's Department of Education.

The primary concern I have from a regulatory perspective is that the University of Phoenix is a bit close to non-compliance with the 90/10 rule.

The 90/10 rule is a federal regulation specific to for-profit colleges. It requires that at least 10% of an institution’s cash-basis revenue come from sources other than Title IV federal student aid (e.g., Pell Grants, Stafford Loans). The other 90% may come from Title IV, but not more. If a school fails the 90/10 test for two consecutive fiscal years, it loses eligibility to participate in Title IV programs. In fiscal 2024, the University of Phoenix reported deriving 88.3% of its revenue from Title IV and other qualifying federal sources. That's a bit too close for comfort.

What allays my concern is that the University of Phoenix is focused these days on increasing its B2B enrollments. In fiscal 2024, about 30% of degreed enrollments came through the B2B channel (students affiliated with corporate/employer partnerships). That’s up from 20% in fiscal 2022, representing a ~32% CAGR in B2B enrollments over that span.

There are other concerns as well. I'm still not comfortable with the company's exposure to borrower's defense to repayment. The S-1 filing discloses that PXED has not accrued any liability for borrower defense claims, noting instead that it “cannot estimate a reasonably possible range of loss.” Given the current policy environment, this risk is less pressing in the near term. It could resurface if the political climate shifts.

The biggest risk for investors is a change in administration in 2028, which could lead to a more aggressive Department of Education stance on for-profit higher ed. That's three years away. If the institution can continue to improve its student outcomes over that period of time, then the risk gets mitigated.

Conclusion

Phoenix Education Partners is priced at a fraction of peer valuations. This presents either an opportunity for a trade on the IPO day or a longer hold period where the stock trades relatively on par with its publicly traded for-profit higher ed peers.

This company has healthy margins, no debt, little regulatory overhang under the Trump Administration, and is priced at a low valuation relative to peers. If the stock is re-rated relative to industry peers, investors can see a material appreciation on the IPO day.

data.title: The Labor Market Continues To Unravel
data.description:
The labor market has been in focus lately, especially since the Fed directly said it was a worry of theirs at the last Federal Open Market Committee ("FOMC") meeting; that's where they decide interest rates. At that meeting, they gave us a 25 bp cut with the promise that they would keep their eyes on the labor market, and their projections showed that they expect to continue with rate cuts into the end of the year.

I've had my eyes on the Bureau of Labor Statistics ("BLS") jobs revisions in particular, since the data they've been collecting this year has been very volatile and led to steep revisions in jobs last month. I was very eager to see the new data coming from the BLS this month. Alas, that data is going to be delayed now that the US government is shut down.

This comes back to the current labor market, which the Fed believes is the more important side of the mandate to attend to currently—I was far more worried about inflation through the summer—then unemployment jumped to 4.3% in August and became the focus of the Fed and financial media alike.

I am still worried about inflation, but it's hard to argue with this chart:

One worry now that the government is shut down is that data won't be made available, as the BLS is not considered essential. While they will still publish the data, it will likely be delayed significantly. This makes the most recent report from the BLS that much more important, as it might be the last one we get for a minute.

Hiring Is Slowing, Openings Are Weak

The Job Openings and Labor Turnover Summary ("JOLTS") report from the BLS was the most recent report on the labor market, coming out on Sept. 30th.

It will be the last report we have from the government until the shutdown is ended and a funding bill is passed.

This most recent JOLTS report shows a continuation of the downward trend in hirings (blue line) that we've been seeing over the last few months, snapping the 2024 trend line that was mostly flat. That comes along with a very soft rise in layoffs, which are still fairly low.

Another way to visualize that data is the ratio of layoffs to openings, which has been steadily declining for over the last few years. We're now in a pretty poor spot for this figure, and it's only accelerating.

Note this following chart is missing August (the report that came out on Sept. 30th), but the most recent figure is at 3.9, matching the September 2024 low.

Month-to-month, job openings rose (19,000) while hirings fell, indicating that businesses are interested in expanding their potential labor pool but are not eager to hire. This cautiousness is congruent with the volatile business climate commentary from earlier and should be expected to some degree.

Overall, openings are down over the last few years and even from a few months ago; this month-to-month win was very small in context.

Here are JOLTS hires on the same timeline for context; also at multi-year lows.

More People Than Jobs

We are also seeing unemployment rise, up to 4.3% (although this is still a good figure overall), at the same time that job openings are falling. This has led to a full reversal of the stimulus-fueled hiring sprees that corporations went on in 2021, when job openings exploded.

To add to the mixed data we're seeing, we may have just witnessed a bottom in this hopeful metric: hiring from non-employment. I am particularly concerned with this metric because of its correlation with Gen Z employment, which is notoriously poor; over 10% of 16-24-year-olds are unemployed, and another 10% are "NEETs." Improvement in this statistic is a good thing for the whole economy, but also for this age cohort in particular, as they are the largest contributor to the non-employed group.

Payroll Data Isn't Any Better

Thankfully for economics wonks like myself, private payroll provider Automated Data Processing (ADP) will continue to provide data on job changes through its own data collection and research even if the government is shut down. Here are the latest month-to-month changes for September:

A lot of hiring at the top and a lot of trimming everywhere else.

That hiring may not be what you think—it's not AI and data center hiring, per se. Only three categories were positive in the ADP payroll data: natural resources and mining, up 3,000; information (there's your AI), up 3,000; and education and health services, up 33,000. The difference is astonishing, but it's not new.

Healthcare was almost all the job growth last year, too, along with leisure and hospitality jobs, although the latter has mostly seen those gains undone this year as tourism, both foreign and domestic, has fallen this year and ended up almost at a net even.

Here's a look at the top employer in each state from 1990 and 2024. That is the look of a maintenance economy. This isn't just a change over the last few months. This has been decades in the making.

The Fed Will Continue Their Path

I doubt the government shutdown will last longer than a few weeks, and so the BLS will be able to produce some figures before the next FOMC meeting for the Fed to decide its path. While that doesn't guarantee that they will have CPI or jobs reports—it will depend on how long the shutdown lasts and how long the data is delayed for.

Regardless, the Fed has access to alternative data sources like the rest of us and certainly sees the weakness indicated in both JOLTS and ADP figures. If ADP continues to show weakness, the Fed won't need JOLTS to confirm that another rate cut is prudent.

Consider that there are far fewer alternative measures for inflation produced outside the BLS that the Fed considers—I can't imagine they are fans of the alternative inflation metrics I've been watching lately.

Another 25 bp cut is ahead of us, by my current guess. I'm not alone in that.

In fact, it seems almost nobody is alone in thinking the Fed will do anything else; there is a 99% chance they cut 25 bp, according to the futures market.

Yield Curve Control Update

One last note on the Fed for now: "third mandate" people rejoice, as the yield curve is starting to show signs of playing along nicely with the Fed. That is to say, lowering the short end of the curve has started to bring down the long end.

Two weeks ago, I called a peak in rates and for folks to take a look at the RFIX ETF following the rate cut and talk of yield curve control. I think the Fed can do it. I hope some of my readers are along for the ride. It's down since then but up since my doubling-down in "The Real Winner of Rate Cuts."

I wrote an article on RFIX in June if you're interested in learning more about it, "Taking the Bond Bull ETF By the Horns." This is one of the holdings that I am gaining more and more confidence in as the curve fully reverted and is now responding as expected to short-end rate cuts.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am still hesitant about the direction of the labor market and do not see volatility subsiding soon. Openings and layoffs are likely to continue their trends, putting the Fed in a position to continue with rate cuts regardless of the data coming out of the BLS halting.

Most interesting about this whole development is that we may finally be seeing long-term rates coming down in response to this softening in the labor market and the expectation of continued Fed cutting. Virtually the entire market foresees a 25 bp cut at the end of the month, and I don't see a reason to disagree currently.

Getting long-term rates in order is just a bonus, and I bet the Fed is really happy about that one taking care of itself. I will continue to bet against them staying high, but I recommend others heed the warnings in the articles linked about my ETF of choice, RFIX, as it requires moderation and prudence to be used effectively.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: AI Capex Should Cripple Next Profit Cycle For S&P 500
data.description:
Any time investing feels like it is easy and also a spectator sport, you have to ask yourself some important questions. Today, it does feel easy, unless you have actually grown up learning finance. For those who were fortunate (or unfortunate, depending on your perspective) to have learned the rules, this time appears surreal. It is as if investors are once again turning a blind eye to the math and saying, "No, this time two plus two does equal two hundred." We look at this equation from the perspective of AI and the capex being sunk in. We ask the questions that need to be asked.

How Much Is Too Much?

When you give big tech valuations that make no sense, they will spend on things that don't make sense. We have had cycles before where capex was high, but this is something else.

It is important to note that from an accounting perspective, capex is investing and stays off the books until the projects are actually put into use. Investors who are following just the profit statement are missing just how much is being tied to the future. Granted these stocks are ridiculously expensive to begin with, even on a pure earnings yield (inverse of P/E) basis, Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), and Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) are very expensive. The 3 sport earnings yields are 3.85%, 1.49%, and 2.62%, respectively. But the picture worsens dramatically when you look at free cash flow yields.

ORCL appears the most expensive of the bunch, and investors are on the hook to justify a negative free cash flow yield. This number is also not revealing the true impact, as it is a trailing 12-month number. The most recent quarter's capex was off the charts. Well, Y-charts did manage to fit it in, but you get the picture.

Based on this run-rate, ORCL now has a negative 3% free cash flow yield. Don't expect this boom to stop until the market reacts negatively to this massive spend.

ORCL will be raising debt left, right, and center, and the $18 billion sale was just a warm-up.

When Will Depreciation Start To Hit The Books?

Of course there are purists out there that will start labelling all these stocks as cheap; as for them, capex is just a down payment on a long runway of growth. But absolutely no one is taking into account just how big a move we will see in depreciation for all these stocks and what that will do to the income statement. ORCL is a good example, as despite revenues being up 12%, net income was flat, and the culprit was depreciation, which was 68% year-over-year.

This depreciation will accelerate to quadruple this run-rate over the next 5 years. This might sound far-fetched, but if we multiply the current depreciation by 4, you still come up short on the quarterly capex run-rate of $8.5 billion. Below we show just how quickly the situation has deteriorated.

The others are not this bad, but the burn rate is fairly intense.

How Will Tech Make Money When AI Is Unprofitable At Today's Prices?

Everyone knows that AI (LLMs, to be accurate) are loss-making ventures today. How investors are making the leap from that to big profits down the line boggles the imagination. This requires an ostrich approach, especially in light of what is happening to pricing.

The revenue concentration comes into sharp focus as OpenAI launched GPT-5 this week with dramatically lower pricing that could undercut Anthropic's premium positioning. Early comparisons show Claude Opus 4 costs roughly seven times more per million tokens than GPT-5 for certain tasks, creating immediate pressure on Anthropic's enterprise pricing strategy and potentially threatening its hard-won dominance in AI coding.

The pricing disparity signals a fundamental shift in competitive dynamics that will force enterprise procurement teams to reconsider vendor relationships built on performance rather than price. Companies managing exponentially growing AI budgets now face comparable capability at a fraction of the cost, creating unavoidable pressure in contract negotiations.

Source: VentureBeat

The current pricing on the US side of the pond looks like this.

Keep those in mind and add this news release from DeepSeek a few days back.

To sweeten the deal, DeepSeek also cut API pricing. Inputs now cost as little as $0.028 per cache hit, down from $0.07, while outputs dropped to $0.42 from $1.68. That pricing move underscores the startup's pitch: powerful models at a fraction of the cost of U.S. rivals.

DeepSeek has been turning heads with its lean training approach. Its reasoning-focused R1 model, for instance, cost just $294,000 to train using slightly more than 500 Nvidia (NVDA) H800 GPUs. By comparison, reports suggest Microsoft-backed OpenAI's GPT-4 burned through more than 10,000 GPUs.

Source: Yahoo Finance

For reference, OpenAI is expected to blow through $8-$14 billion in 2025 and 2026 while charging 25 times DeepSeek's output price.

The entire industry is in a deflationary revenue-per-user spiral while costs keep exploding.

Verdict

Reality is coming for this indiscriminate spending, and it will impact profits over many years. OpenAI may be the most immune, as it has not gotten investors even used to the idea of a positive income statement. Everything released from the company about the future of AI can be summed up in value in the chart below.

The rest of Big tech looks cheaper than the year 2000, but that is precisely because they are using their base business to finance this experiment. Ultimately there is nothing new here that will be able to sell outside of a slightly improved mousetrap for trillions of capex.

S&P 500 has become incredibly risky with extreme concentration risk.

Buyer Beware.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Borr Drilling: Mexico Momentum And Middle East Demand Drive Growth
data.description:
High Utilization, But Margin Pressures Persist

Borr Drilling Limited (NYSE:BORR) operates a fleet of shallow-water jack-up rigs for the offshore oil and gas producers. The company operates in the Middle East, Asia, and Latin America. Borr is positioned for steady growth, supported by high jack-up utilization and expanding international contracts. It aims to balance international expansion with strong liquidity. Following the recent policy upgrades, Mexico’s renewed push for production and private investment adds upside.

On the other hand, rig retirements tighten supply and limit oilfield services’ scale of operations. In this environment, BORR’s strong liquidity and disciplined spending are the key factors that will determine its ability to weather near-term volatility. Based on its long-duration work secured, I expect day rates and margins to remain resilient into 2026. Despite the stock’s relative overvaluation, a positive outlook and strong drivers validate my “Buy” call for the stock.

Industry Outlook

Year 2025 marks a volatile year for the energy sector as global conflicts, tariff uncertainty, and OPEC’s rollback of production cuts keep investors on the swivel. Brent briefly hit a high in June (25% up since May) as Middle East tensions raised supply concerns. But the momentum tapered off as OPEC production cuts reversed amidst a shaky global demand. It adversely affected day rates following the Saudi rig suspensions, although jack-up utilization stayed firm above 90%.

Despite the pressure, most of the idle rigs are being absorbed, with new demand in the Middle East expected to take up the rest by 2026–27. It appears that the frac activity is sliding. The frac spread count, according to Primary Vision, is already down 12% from Q2. In Mexico, some units are estimated to have been retired, and several others are being held for sale.

Company Outlook

Given the backdrop, Borr expects Q3 activity and performance to be similar to Q2, as the management’s EBITDA estimates are close to $470 million. Mexico’s renewed support for Pemex and its 1.8 million bpd target should improve liquidity and open more drilling opportunities. Private investment projects could also provide upside for Borr in Mexico.

Near-term volatility may persist, but long-term jack-up fundamentals look strong. Global energy demand is growing; shallow water projects remain attractive. With no new builds on the horizon and an aging fleet, I expect utilization to remain high and support steady day rates.

Mexico’s Significance And Other Projects

Mexico is becoming Borr’s key market. The country is boosting Pemex’s liquidity with large debt financings and reaffirming its production goal, which should support drilling activity. Borr is well placed to win incremental work, including private projects that are set to become a bigger part of Mexico’s output. At the same time, rig retirements are picking up, tightening supply and adding support to market fundamentals.

Borr added 14 new contracts in 2025, boosting backlog by $318 million and extending earnings visibility. Recent wins in Vietnam, the Middle East, and Mexico highlight strong utilization. The company has secured rigs with long-duration work that could lift day rates. Despite a suspension notice for one rig in June, the steady flow of new awards underlines Borr’s ability to limit idle time and strengthen its regional positions.

Risk Factors

Global instability, from the war in Ukraine to tensions in the Middle East and Asia, continues to create uncertainty for oil and gas markets. Industry capital spending has been volatile, with gradual cuts in capex over the past few years and a collapse in oil prices in 2020, followed by a recovery. But the price swings have been ongoing.

Also, higher oil prices don’t always translate into sustained drilling, as operators can still cancel or defer programs based on exploration results, capital access, or other priorities. Regional differences also matter, with higher-cost basins being slower to recover and rig relocations leading to tighter competition in matured markets.

My Estimates

Over the past 10 quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 12% on average. I expect Borr’s EBITDA margin to edge higher or stay flat through mid-2026. Strong utilization and contract visibility should drive profitability, although the temporary slowdown in Mexico or higher rig maintenance costs can put the brake on it.

Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 11%–13%. Given BORR’s trailing-12-month adjusted EBITDA of $481 million, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase to ~$540 million in the next four quarters.

Analyzing Q2 Drivers

From Q1 to Q2 (as released in its Q2 2025 earnings dated August 13), BORR’s revenue rose by 24%, and EBITDA jumped 39%. Approximately 22 of its 24 rigs were active, pushing technical utilization at 99.6% in Q2.

As I discussed above, a multi-rig contract in Asia and a new contract for the Arabia II can bolster BORR’s fleet utilization. Contract coverage reached 84% for 2025, with solid day rates. More operating days for the Vale and Tor (modern jack-up rigs) improved day rate revenue in Q2. Other factors pushing the Q2 sales higher were new starts in Brazil and Mexico and higher management contract revenue from the Galar recommencement.

Cash Flows And Balance Sheet

Borr's cash flow from operations increased to $145 million in 6M 2025 from $40 million a year ago. Capex increased from $22 million to $39 million due to jack-up maintenance and rig activations. So, free cash flow also increased from $18 million a year ago to $107 million in 1H 2025. The rise in cash flows relates to an increase in the amount due to higher receivables from Pemex and other parties.

Upon diving deep into the receivable issue, we note that the company has $60 million to $65 million of outstanding payments from Mexico. Note that a rise in receivables adds to working capital and cash flow from operations but also increases the chance of non-receipt or delayed receipt. However, recent government financing efforts are expected to ease the situation. The company now expects payments against the receivable to start flowing from Q3. Also, the company’s accrued revenue has risen while rig rates have increased versus last quarter. As of June 30, 2025, BORR's liquidity (cash balance plus available credit facility) was $261 million.

Recently, Borr Drilling raised $102.5 million in equity and expanded its credit facilities. It boosted its liquidity by $200 million. The move aims to strengthen the balance sheet and support long-term strategic goals.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

BORR's forward EV/EBITDA multiple versus the current multiple is expected to expand versus an unchanged multiple for its peers. This usually results in a lower EV/EBITDA multiple compared to its peers. However, its EV/EBITDA multiple (5.8x) is higher than its peers' (PDS, SDRL, and PTEN) average of 5.1x. So, the stock is relatively overvalued.

BORR's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 74x. I believe the industry dynamics have undergone a complete transformation, and the stock will not trade at a similar multiple anytime soon. If it trades at the peers' average, the stock price can decrease by 33%. This, also, seems unlikely because BORR’s growth outlook is more robust than its peers.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 11%-13% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current EV/EBITDA multiple of 5.8x, I think the stock should trade between $4.2 and $4.4, implying a 57% upside. Although the stock appears overvalued, I think the 29% fall over the past year has rendered it attractive to investors.

Wall Street Rating

Two sell-side analysts rated BORR a "buy" (including a "strong buy"), three analysts rated it a "hold," and none rated it a "sell." The consensus target price is $3.13, suggesting a 1% upside at the current price. Given the company's operational recovery, I think Wall Street analysts are underestimating the return potential.

What's The Take On BORR?

I see indications of an international drilling activity slowdown on the horizon. BORR’s jack-up utilization remains strong, but day rates are under pressure from Saudi drilling suspensions. Frac activity is sliding, and rig retirements in Mexico are picking up.

On the positive side, Mexico is becoming a key growth market as Pemex and private projects see additional investments. The company added 14 new contracts in Q2, which will boost the backlog and improve visibility. It has also secured long-duration work in Asia and the Middle East, supporting utilization and day rates.

The company’s cash flow surged in 6M 2025. A recent equity raise and credit expansion strengthened its liquidity. In my view, the company’s Q3 and Q4 still reflect margin strain, but the setup for 2026 looks more constructive, backed by Pemex’s renewed traction in Mexico. Although the stock’s relative valuation looks stretched, weighing all the factors, I recommend a “Buy.”
